Please describe your research career to date
Since 2021, I have secured a total of £118,128 in personal research grants and been lead investigator for three
clinical and three lab-based studies.
2021-2022
In 2021 I was awarded the competitive Royal Brompton and Harefield Hospitals (RBHT) Charity research
fellowship (£43,175). In collaboration with Dr MacBean, Professor Polkey and experts at Aalborg University,
Denmark we designed a novel observational study to understand the physiological response to rehabilitation and
Inspiratory Muscle Training (IMT) in mechanically ventilated patients in intensive care (ICU). IMT is an exercise
aimed at improving breathing muscle strength to help critically ill patients breathe without a ventilator.
In leading this seven-month stud

secarb__001_b.text:
y, I doubled the original recruitment target from 20 to 40, conducting 106
measurements. I was responsible for all day-to-day management including recruitment, data analysis and
manuscript preparation. The study involved a challenging three-hour exercise protocol in mechanically ventilated
patients, collecting specialised physiological data. The results described which patients might better tolerate IMT in
ICU. I presented these data at the American Thoracic Society international conference, 2023. The results are now
published (first author) in Intensive Care Medicine Experimental (IF 3.5). I was awarded “Directors’ Choice” prize in
the annual RBHT research showcase for this work. A further manuscript reporting secondary outcomes is under
peer review.
To broaden my research skills, I conducted two additional lab studies within the fellowship to build on results of the
ICU study. I organised a one-week visit to Aalborg University, where I worked with academics to conduct a robust
IMT research protocol. We measured oxygen consumption during four different IMT loads in ten participants. After
completing a coding course, I will analyse these data later in my PCAF during a second visit.

secarb__001_c.text:
Brunel Partners Academic Centre for Health Sciences funded a second healthy study (£1000) to measure load
perception in addition to oxygen consumption during IMT. Under the supervision of Dr MacBean, I supported two
MSc students in study techniques and data collection at Brunel University, where we recruited 30 participants in
two months. I analysed the data in collaboration with Aalborg University. I supported the MSc students to present
two abstracts at The Physiological Society conference, 2023. I am first author on the manuscript in preparation.
2023-2024
In 2023 I moved to the Harefield Respiratory Research Group to enhance my research skills and knowledge in
larger, NIHR-funded trials. I was the lead investigator in a trial investigating a novel app for patients attending
pulmonary rehabilitation. I was responsible for all day-to-day running of the trial, where I recruited 69 patients
(target: 50) in four months. I led the data analysis and am first author for the manuscript in preparation. I wrote up
data previously presented at the European Respiratory Society congress (funded by a Council for Allied Health
Professions Research conference award), now published as a full ar

secarb__001_d.text:
ticle in ERJ Open Research (IF 4.6).
In addition to my full-time role, I was the principal investigator on an observational study investigating a
modification to reduce oxygen consumption in ventilators, in collaboration with clinical engineering and Professor
Polkey. I was responsible for all aspects of the trial, including ethics, recruitment, and a complex two-hour study
procedure in thirteen mechanically ventilated patients. I led the analysis and manuscript preparation (currently
under peer review).
2024
The NIHR PCAF has enabled extensive PPI activity, PPI training (Public Involvement in Research, Imperial
College London (ICL)), and to conduct a lab study to inform specific elements of my DCAF. Under the supervision
of Dr MacBean, I learnt techniques in respiratory electromyography (EMG). I conducted a complex EMG study in
healthy participants to refine the protocol used in study 1 of this DCAF.
I attended MSc modules in qualitative and quantitative research methods (Brunel University) and in clinical
exercise (UCL). I will attend a five-day researcher development course at UCL, and a five-day statistics for medical
research course at ICL later in my PCAF. These formal course

secarb__001_e.text:
s have increased my broader understanding of
research design, statistics and methodology which lead well into the programme of work in my DCAF. I convened
a PPI group with which I have actively engaged throughout planning this DCAF.
Professional experience
I am a highly specialist respiratory physiotherapist with an interest in physical rehabilitation, weaning and
respiratory rehabilitation after critical illness. I have over six years’ experience working at the RBHT specialising in
critical care and surgery, building on extensive knowledge derived from five years as an ICU nurse.
I realise development as a clinical academic involves more than just conducting research. Clinically, I regularly
teach physiotherapists and medics in critical care. I am an honorary research fellow at Brunel University and
lecture on two critical care advanced clinical practice MSc modules. I attended the Starting Out in Research
course at ICL in 2020, and was subsequently asked to mentor on the course, a role I continue.
Under the supervision of Professor McWilliams, I am forging a clinical academic role at Harefield Hospital, where I
have championed research by supporting colleagues with audits, servic

secarb__001_f.text:
e evaluations and quality improvement
projects. These will lead to our work being presented at Trust research events and submitted to conferences.
As an editorial board member at the Association of Chartered Physiotherapists in Respiratory Care, I am leading a
team of 11 to conduct an IMT scoping review of over 2900 articles, to be published next year. This role has
provided an up-to-date review of the IMT literature and supported my development through leadership and
networking.
Future aspirations
I aspire to be a leading clinical academic with an international profile in weaning and rehabilitation in critical care.
The training, practice supervision and skills I develop during this DCAF will enable me to conduct further research
and embed a research culture into clinical practice to improve patient care. Within one year of completion, I would
apply for postdoctoral funding to conduct a definitive RCT from the results of this DCAF. The strong track record
for supporting clinical academic careers at RBHT/GSTT mean I am well positioned for this progression.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Purpose
200,000 adults are admitted to intensive care in the UK each year. Many of these patients need help with their
breathing and are put on a machine called a ventilator.
Research shows that many patients using a ventilator quickly develop weakness in their main breathing muscle,
called the diaphragm. This weakness can cause a longer time on the ventilator, a longer hospital stay, and higher
chances of health problems or death.
Inspiratory muscle training is a breathing exercise where the patient breathes against resistance through a small
device (like breathing through a straw). It aims to improve the strength of the diaphragm and reduce how long
patients are on the ventilator.
Why is this research needed?
Research shows that current breathing exercise programmes do not help patients come off the ventilator or go
home quicker. This may be because these breathing exercises strengthen the wrong muscles (those in the chest
and neck, that are not efficient). These exercises can also be distressing for patients.
This research aims to develop a breathing exercise programme that focusses on strengthening the diaphragm. We
will also make sure patients do not find the breathing exercis
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
e too distressing. We will then test if it is possible to
carry out a large study to see if this programme can help patients come off the ventilator and go home quicker.
What will be done in this research?
Two studies will be done on patients who cannot breathe without a ventilator.
Study 1:
Will measure how the breathing muscles respond to four different levels of breathing exercise and ask patients to
feedback on each level.
1. At each breathing exercise level, the effort of the breathing muscles will be measured using a small tube
inserted into the nostril and small sticky patches on the chest and neck. We aim to find the level where the
diaphragm is being pushed to work hard but other muscles in the chest and neck are not.
2. After each breathing exercise level patients will be asked how they found it – for example hard, easy,
uncomfortable, scary, motivating.
The results from both these parts will together help to make a breathing exercise programme which
focusses on strengthening the diaphragm and is acceptable to patients.
Study 2:
Will test if it is possible to carry out a large enough study to see if the breathing exercise programme developed in
study 1 works. We will aim
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
to find out if it is possible to recruit and keep patients in the study.
Patients will be randomly put into one of two groups by a computer:
 One group will perform the breathing exercise programme formed in study 1 (they will perform 60 breaths
a day through the device) and usual care.
One group will have usual care.

Patients will stay in the study until they can breathe without the ventilator.
We will also interview physiotherapists to ask how they felt doing these breathing exercises with patients.
How will patients be involved?
Patients who have spent a long time on a ventilator in intensive care have helped design this study. They will
continue to be involved when the study is running. They will help design information sheets, consent forms and
continue to advise on how to ask patients about their experience of breathing exercises. One person from the
group will attend scientific meetings with the researchers.
Telling people about the results of the study
We will tell healthcare professionals about the results at conferences, and by publishing them in journals. We will
work with patient advisory groups and charities to inform patients using blogs, newsletters, and social me
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secsa__001_a.text:
Background
Diaphragm weakness is present in 63-80% of patients mechanically ventilated for over 48 hours and is associated
with increased length of intensive care and hospital stay, greater cognitive and functional impairments and
increased mortality risk.
The diaphragm is responsible for 80% of work in normal breathing, therefore specific diaphragm strengthening
may help patients wean from mechanical ventilation. 63% of intensive care physiotherapists worldwide use
Inspiratory Muscle Training (IMT), which aims to improve diaphragm strength through the application of resistance
during inspiration, using a device connected directly to the patient’s tracheostomy. However, there are a number of
issues with current IMT approaches in intensive care. There is no consensus on how to specifically train the
diaphragm. High-intensity IMT will substantially recruit extra-diaphragmatic inspiratory muscles and type IIb
(power) diaphragm muscle fibres; neither are useful for ventilator weaning. Current approaches to IMT in
mechanically ventilated patients do not improve clinical outcomes, and can be unpleasant and distressing for
patients.
Research question
Can a Diaphragm-focussed and patient-a

secsa__001_b.text:
cceptable Inspiratory Muscle Training load (D-IMT) be identified and
tested in difficult-to-wean, mechanically ventilated patients?
Aims
1. Develop a diaphragm-focussed and patient-acceptable IMT load (D-IMT).
2. Test the feasibility of conducting a randomised controlled trial comparing the D-IMT load with usual care.
Objectives
Study 1
1. Measure diaphragmatic and extra-diaphragmatic inspiratory muscle activity during four IMT loads.
2. Explore the acceptability of these different IMT loads using qualitative methods in line with participants’
communication ability.
3. Integrate quantitative and qualitative data to determine a diaphragm-focussed and patient acceptable IMT load
(D-IMT)
Study 2
Conduct a feasibility randomised controlled trial (RCT) to ascertain if it is possible to conduct a definitive RCT to
test the clinical- and cost-effectiveness of D-IMT compared to usual care.
Methods and timelines
Study 1 (months 1-17).
An observational, mixed methods study. Forty difficult-to-wean, mechanically ventilated participants will perform
ten breaths of IMT at 15, 25, 35 and 45% of their maximal inspiratory strength on up to three occasions. During
IMT, diaphragm activity will be me

secsa__001_c.text:
asured using an oesophageal electromyography (EMG) catheter. Extra-
diaphragmatic inspiratory muscle activity will be measured using surface EMG. Participant experience of each load
will be explored qualitatively. These data will be integrated to inform a diaphragm-focussed, patient-acceptable
IMT load (D-IMT).
Study 2 (months 18-36). Feasibility RCT. Fifty difficult-to-wean, mechanically ventilated participants will be
randomised to usual care or D-IMT and usual care. The D-IMT group will perform the IMT load defined in study 1
five days per week until they no longer require mechanical ventilation. Feasibility outcomes: eligible patients,
recruitment rates, completeness of outcome measures, IMT fidelity, patient adherence to IMT, participant
retention, adverse events. Semi-structured interviews will be conducted on physiotherapists who administered D-
IMT to explore acceptability of the intervention.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
Findings will be disseminated via peer-reviewed publications, conference presentations and patient and public
events in collaboration with the project’s PPI group. Results of study 2 will inform a definitive RCT with the
potential to improve clinic

secsa__001_d.text: al and patient-related outcomes and reduce NHS costs.

secdrp__001_a.text:
What is the problem being addressed?
Diaphragm dysfunction in mechanically ventilated patients
Around 200,000 adult patients are admitted to intensive care (ICU) in the UK each year1, of whom 50% require
invasive mechanical ventilation2. Diaphragmatic dysfunction (weakness) is present in 63-80% of patients
mechanically ventilated for over 48 hours, and is associated with prolonged ventilation, increased mortality, and
readmission to ICU3-8. Patients who require prolonged ventilation (‘difficult to wean’ patients) have increased long-
term cognitive and functional impairments, and poorer post-discharge survival7,9 Difficult to wean patients occupy
.
a disproportionate number of ICU bed days, with increased healthcare costs10,11.
Inspiratory muscle training
Inspiratory Muscle Training (IMT) has been proposed as treatment strategy for difficult to wean patients12. IMT
aims to improve the strength and endurance of the diaphragm through the application of resistance during
inspiration13,14, using a small device which is connected directly to the patient’s tracheostomy.
However, although IMT has been shown to improve overall volitional inspiratory muscle strength (of the diaphragm
and th

secdrp__001_b.text:
e extra-diaphragmatic muscles), two systematic reviews have concluded there is insufficient evidence to
confirm if it has a positive effect on clinical outcomes such as weaning duration, and ICU and hospital length of
stay14,15 There is currently no consensus on how to specifically train the diaphragm adequately in mechanically
ventilated patients16, and significant heterogeneity in training protocols reported in the literature, with no clear
physiological basis for training regimes chosen16-23.
The diaphragm is the primary respiratory muscle24, yet frequently cited IMT loads will recruit non-diaphragmatic
inspiratory muscles, not useful for sustained respiratory effort in mechanically ventilated patients 16,25,26.
Additionally, mechanically ventilated patients experience moderate to severe breathlessness during loaded
breathing in ICU27; a sensation consistently associated with the fear of dying28. IMT is unpleasant and distressing
for patients; members of the PPI group for this project describe high load IMT in ICU “like dying” or “it was an
extremely hard task”. One member quoted that “recollections of doing IMT formed hallucinations, dreams and
nightmares which I experienced du

secdrp__001_c.text:
ring and after my ICU stay”. There are calls for the optimisation of the IMT
stimulus in difficult to wean patients,29,30 using the principles of muscle physiology and exercise prescription16.
This project will address these problems by:
1. Developing a diaphragm focussed and patient acceptable IMT regime (D-IMT) using an embedded mixed
method approach.
2. A randomised feasibility study will determine whether it is possible to conduct a larger randomised
controlled trial (RCT) implementing D-IMT compared to usual care.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
service users and health and social care services?
Consequences of diaphragm weakness
Diaphragm weakness is a marker of illness severity and poor prognosis in mechanically ventilated patients3,31,32.
Prolonged ventilation (associated with diaphragm weakness) impacts patients significantly, and is associated with
disability at six months post-ICU discharge, activity limitation including return to work, reduced quality of life, and
impairments7,9,33,34.
cognitive
The patient experience of IMT
The most commonly used IMT regimes13 are unpleasant for patients, who can

secdrp__001_d.text:
experience severe breathlessness
during and after IMT in ICU. Breathlessness is a major stressor for mechanically ventilated patients35, which can
contribute to post-traumatic stress syndrome in ICU survivors36.
Healthcare resources
The optimal IMT load to use in clinical practice which improves clinical outcomes is yet to be defined14,29,30.
ICU37.
However, 63% of physiotherapists worldwide administer IMT in A survey I conducted through Brunel
University found 50% of UK physiotherapists use IMT in ICU, meaning NHS resources are being used to deliver
an intervention lacking a robust evidence base.
These data show the importance of this research. Finding the optimal approach to IMT may:
1. Help patients wean from mechanical ventilation quicker. This could ameliorate the negative effects of
prolonged ventilation to improve outcomes, and reduce NHS costs through a reduction in ICU and hospital
bed days.
2. Improve patient wellbeing in ICU by reducing stressors associated with IMT.
3. Improve resource use by informing clinical practice with robust evidence.
Review of existing evidence
Seven RCTs have investigated IMT in mechanically ventilated patients (Table 1). There is substantial
h

secdrp__001_e.text:
eterogeneity in loads used, set anywhere between 20 and 50% of the patient’s inspiratory strength, then
increased to a load where the patient can just complete the sixth breath of IMT17-23

Two systematic reviews concluded that while IMT may improve volitional measures of overall inspiratory muscle
strength, this does not translate to improvements in meaningful clinical outcomes such as weaning duration or
success14,15.
Justification for a physiologically informed, acceptable load
The diaphragm is the primary respiratory muscle, responsible for around 80% of respi

secdrp__001_f.text:
ratory work, recruiting mainly
type I and IIa endurance fibres in normal breathing in healthy adults16,24. Importantly, it is specific diaphragm
weakness that is strongly associated with weaning failure in mechanically ventilated patients, not overall
inspiratory strength (encompassing the diaphragm and extra-diaphragmatic inspiratory muscles)3,32,38. IMT will not
only target the diaphragm, but to a larger extent the extra-diaphragmatic inspiratory muscles as the load
increases39. High load IMT is frequently used in difficult to wean patients18,20,21,23.
During my PCAF, under the supervision of Dr MacBean we collected novel data during IMT at eight loads in
healthy subjects. As IMT load increases, there is a proportionally greater increase in extra-diaphragmatic muscle
activation compared to diaphragmatic activation at higher IMT loads.
High load IMT causes a training effect on the scalene and sternocleidomastoid muscles (extra-diaphragmatic
muscles), but does not improve weaning success29, questioning the utility of high load IMT. Although the extra-
diaphragmatic muscles are a mechanism of compensation that can be activated in the presence of increased
respiratory load (for examp

secdrp__001_g.text:
le, during exercise), they are not useful for sustained ventilatory effort in mechanically
ventilated patients25,26. Greater activation of extra-diaphragmatic inspiratory muscles and concurrent diaphragmatic
dysfunction are associated with an increased likelihood of failing a ventilator weaning trial26,40,41.
These data emphasise the importance of limiting extra-diaphragmatic muscle activation breathing in
critically ill patients and build a case for targeted diaphragmatic IMT in difficult to wean patients.
The importance of patient experience and feedback in defining the optimal IMT load cannot be underestimated
and is why this study uses a mixed methods approach. IMT involves the physiotherapist connecting the IMT
device directly to the patient’s tracheostomy. The patient is compelled to breathe through the applied resistance to
obtain tidal volume. An acceptable load would avoid distressing experiences for patients as previously
reported27,35,
and as highlighted by the project PPI group. It may also reduce IMT refusal rates, potentially
improving the effectiveness of the intervention. Percentage of intended IMT sessions completed varies from 30 to
88% in the literature18,21. Ref

secdrp__001_h.text:
usal or fatigue are frequent reasons for not completing IMT21, which may be related to
the unpleasant nature of IMT.
Research question
Can a Diaphragm-focussed and patient-acceptable Inspiratory Muscle Training load (D-IMT) be identified and
tested in difficult to wean, mechanically ventilated patients?
Aims
1. Develop a diaphragm-focussed and patient-acceptable IMT load (D-IMT).
2. Test the feasibility of conducting a randomised controlled trial (RCT) comparing the D-IMT load with usual care.
Objectives
Study 1
1. Measure diaphragmatic and extra-diaphragmatic (scalene, sternocleidomastoid, and pectoral) inspiratory
muscle activation using electromyography (EMG) during four IMT loads in difficult to wean patients.
2. Explore the acceptability of these different IMT loads using intensity and unpleasantness numeric rating scales
and qualitative methods in line with their communication needs.
3. Integrate quantitative and qualitative data to determine a diaphragm-focussed and patient acceptable IMT load
(D-IMT).
Study 2
Conduct a feasibility RCT to determine if it is possible to conduct a definitive RCT to test the clinical- and cost-
effectiveness of D-IMT compared to usual care. Res

secdrp__001_i.text:
ults will guide the design and powering of a definitive RCT,
aligning with the Medical Research Council guidance on complex interventions42.
Project plan
The research support service and a statistician were consulted in the design of this plan.
Study 1: A prospective, mixed methods, observational study to define a diaphragm-focussed and patient-
acceptable IMT load (D-IMT) in difficult to wean, mechanically ventilated patients.
(Months 1-17)
Design
The complexity of IMT in mechanically ventilated patients who have compromised means to communicate
warrants an embedded mixed methods approach; Neither a purely qualitative nor quantitative approach would
gain sufficient understanding of the problem. In this design quantitative and qualitative data will be collected
simultaneously, embedding qualitative data within quantitative data. This approach allows the focus to be on the
quantitative data necessary to determine the parameters for diaphragm-focussed IMT whilst the qualitative data
experiences43.
will provide crucial insights into patients’
Participants will perform four IMT loads, where diaphragmatic and extra-diaphragmatic muscle activation will be
measured. After each of the four

secdrp__001_j.text:
IMT loads, and the entire session, qualitative data will be collected. It is
recognised that, due to delirium or impaired cognition, there will be variability in participants’ ability to engage in
qualitative inquiry about their experience. The PPI group felt strongly that data collection over 1-3 occasions would
improve data quality, which will also account for changes in condition, cognition and weaning status, as previously
described44. Patients meeting eligibility criteria will not be excluded due to confusion or impaired cognition at
consent.
Figure 1 details the design of study 1.
Inclusion criteria
 Mechanically ventilated patients in whom weaning has not been achieved within 24 hours after a first
weaning attempt7
 Aged ≥ 18 years
 Co-operative and able to participate in IMT.
 Informed consent or surrogate approval.
Exclusion criteria
 Facial or skull fracture, or a bleeding disorder which would prohibit oesophageal catheter placement.
 Undrained pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum.
 Pregnancy
 Unlikely to survive, or consultant assessment that the patient is not appropriate.
 Patient requiring internal or external cardiac pacing.
 Wounds that prohibit EMG electrode p

secdrp__001_k.text:
lacement.
Consent
By preference, informed consent will be sought. However, some patients will be able to participate in IMT but not
fully understand the study to give informed consent, due to delirium or sedation. In such cases, surrogate consent
will be sought from a personal consultee, and retrospective consent sought when capacity is regained. These
arrangements are how I conducted my previous IMT study in ICU45 and will be subject to ethical approval.
Sample size
This is the first study of its kind to measure inspiratory muscle activation and explore patient experience during
different IMT loads in mechanically ventilated patients. No formal sample size calculation has been performed;
however, an RSS statistician has advised. Based on data from the healthy study in my PCAF, and accounting for
the anticipated heterogeneity in the ICU population, we hypothesise that studying 40 participants on 1-3 occasions
will allow adequate precision. This target is feasible in a 12-month recruitment window; during my charity
fellowship I recruited 40 mechanically ventilated patients with similar eligibility criteria, conducting 106
measurements in geographically different locations over seven

secdrp__001_l.text:
-months.
Data collection (Months 2-13)
Quantitative data collection methods
Inspiratory muscle training
IMT will be performed using a PowerBreathe KH2 inspiratory muscle trainer. The device delivers a specific
patients29,46.
resistance during inspiration and is safe in mechanically ventilated Participants will be disconnected
from the ventilator, connected to the IMT device, and asked to inhale against the resistance 10 times. This
procedure of disconnecting patients from ventilation is cited in all ICU IMT literature, with few adverse events
reported, and all non-serious14,29,46.
Participants will perform IMT at 15, 25, 35 and 45% of their maximal inspiratory pressure in random order, with 20
minutes between loads to allow for recovery and qualitative data collection. The IMT loads arise directly from the
study conducted during my PCAF, where we tested eight IMT loads in healthy individuals. We noted a proportional
increase in diaphragm activation with increasing IMT load, which, from 30-40% of maximal inspiratory strength
was accompanied by a proportionally greater increase in extra-diaphragmatic muscle activity. Testing around this
range will allow us to identify a load which ma

secdrp__001_m.text:
ximises diaphragm activation up to the highest level achievable, but
below the upward inflection point of extra-diaphragmatic muscle activity.
Measurement of inspiratory muscle activation during IMT
Diaphragm activation will be measured using an oesophageal multipair EMG catheter (NAVA, Getinge, Sweden)
designed for mechanically ventilated patients. The catheter also acts as a nasogastric feeding tube; therefore, any
existing nasogastric tube will be removed with agreement of the clinical team. Activation of extra-diaphragmatic
inspiratory muscles (scalene, sternocleidomastoid, and pectoral) will be measured using surface EMG. I learnt the
required skills for oesophageal catheter placement, EMG data collection, and EMG analysis during my PCAF.
Diaphragm and extra-diaphragmatic EMG is feasible47-49 and relates to effort in mechanically ventilated patients47.
Perceived difficultly and unpleasantness.
After each IMT load, perceived breathing difficulty and unpleasantness will be measured using a 0-10 numeric
rating scale, as previously described in ICU28 with anchor points informed by the project’s PPI group: “0=none”,
“10=extreme”.
Qualitative data collection methods
Participant expe

secdrp__001_n.text:
rience will be explored after each IMT load. Recognising the challenges of communicating with
participants in ICU, extensive PPI engagement and collaboration with the ICU lead speech and language therapist
has informed this element of this study.
Participants will have a spectrum of communication abilities, therefore a range of methods will be employed,
developed with the ICU lead speech and language therapist and informed by evidence44,50,51:
 Inline speaking valve
 Above cuff vocalisation
 Bespoke communication boards
 Written communication
 Lip-reading
 Electrolarynx
Participants will have varying degrees of abilities to impart anything from basic to complex information50,
influenced by weakness, cognition, delirium, fatigue and sedation52. For this reason, a generic qualitative inquiry
approach will be used to respond to the different needs and abilities of participants. The generic qualitative
approach aims at understanding how individuals make meaning of a phenomenon or situation. In this pragmatic
approach, a ‘toolbox’ of data collection methods will be used. Extensive and ongoing discussions with the project’s
PPI group have helped inform the content and method of dat

secdrp__001_o.text:
a collection, including:
1. Semi-structured interviews
2. Informal interviews
3. Picture and word boards depicting words used by the PPI group to describe IMT, such as “Fear,
apprehension, anxiety, fatigue, extremely difficult, chest tight, very tired, confidence, positive, effort,
pleasant”.
Semi-structured and informal interviews been successfully performed previously in mechanically ventilated and
recently weaned patients in ICU44. Qualitative data will be recorded using a voice recorder. Fieldwork notes
detailing contextual factors and patient observation will provide supplementary data to improve the richness of the
qualitative data.
Data analysis (Months 14-15)
Quantitative
Mean peak per-breath activity EMG of inspiratory muscle activation will be presented as percentage contribution of
the diaphragm versus extra-diaphragmatic inspiratory muscles. Data will be tested for normality and presented
accordingly. Percentage activation for each IMT load will be compared using a one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis
test.
The aim is to identify the IMT load with the maximum diaphragmatic contribution to breathing and an acceptable
perceived level of unpleasantness. This will be achieved th

secdrp__001_p.text:
rough visual inspection of individual plots of percentage
diaphragmatic activity against load, as per my PCAF study. “Acceptable” unpleasantness will be informed by the
qualitative data.
Qualitative
Audio-recordings of qualitative data will be transcribed verbatim (using a professional service in line with GDPR),
anonymised and managed through NVivo software V12.6.1 and analysed using the six steps of Reflexive
Thematic Analysis53. This involves coding at sentence level and developing categories and overarching themes
while remaining firmly wedded to the data and context. My supervisor (CK) will independently code one transcript
for comparison and validation of themes. Fieldnotes will be drawn upon to provide additional contextual
information during the analysis process, this will be particularly important for participants with limited ability to
communicate beyond a few words at a time.
Integration of quantitative and qualitative data
Following the separate analysis of the quantitative and qualitative data sets, the data will be integrated and
presented in side-by-side joint displays (agreement, partial agreement and silence) to ascertain new insights
beyond the information obtain

secdrp__001_q.text:
ed from the separate quantitative and qualitative results54,55.
Study 2: A feasibility RCT implementing a diaphragm-focussed, patient-acceptable IMT load (D-IMT)
compared to usual care
(Months 18-36)
Design
A single centre randomised controlled feasibility study (Figure 1). Difficult to wean, mechanically ventilated
patients will be randomised to receive either the D-IMT load (informed by study 1) or usual care. Participants will
remain in the study until they are successfully weaned from mechanical ventilation.
D-IMT group: Participants will receive usual care and perform IMT 5 days a week: 6 sets of 10 breaths at the load
identified in study 1, administered by a costed physiotherapists to ensure adequate blinding for secondary
outcome measures.
Control group: Usual care
Randomisation
Participants will be randomised 1:1 to D-IMT or usual care after consent, using a web-based randomisation system
provided by REDCap, by a costed physiotherapist for allocation concealment.
Data collection (Months 18-32)
Feasibility outcomes:
 Number of eligible patients
 Recruitment (willingness of eligible patients to consent, reason for non-enrolment)
 Rate of clinician refusal
 Protocol compli

secdrp__001_r.text:
ance (completeness of secondary outcome measures)
 Intervention fidelity (participant adherence to D-IMT; percentage of intended breaths, sessions and load
performed – logged on the PowerBreathe IMT device)
 Study retention (percentage of withdrawals over study period)
 Adverse events in both groups
Secondary (clinical) outcome measures
Patient-centred outcomes
Patient-centred outcomes require greater attention in trials investigating IMT in mechanically ventilated patients14.
Remarkably, just one IMT study has measured patient-centred outcomes in mechanically ventilated patients21.
survivors35,
Breathlessness is the second most recalled traumatic experience in ICU highlighting its importance as
an outcome measure. Extensive work with the project’s PPI group identified weaning time, ICU and hospital length
of stay, quality of life, breathlessness, and physical function as important secondary outcome measures.
 Weaning duration Defined as time from initiation of mechanical ventilation to successful weaning of
mechanical ventilation (48 hours of unassisted breathing32)
 ICU and hospital length of stay
 Quality of life. Measured one-week after successful weaning using the SF-36

secdrp__001_s.text:
and EQ-5D-3L. The SF-36
is reliable, is responsive, and has construct and criterion validity in ICU survivors56. The EQ-5D-3L tool has
survivors57.
been used extensively in ICU
 Breathlessness. Measured on enrolment and after successful weaning using a 0-10 numeric rating scale,
as previously described28 with anchor points informed by the project’s PPI group: “0=none”, “10=extreme”.
Participants will be asked to report their average and peak breathlessness experienced in the last 24h21.
 Function. Measured one-week after successful weaning using the Chelsea critical care physical
assessment tool (CPAx), which measures respiratory function, functional ability and grip strength. It has
construct and cross-sectional validity across the ICU and hospital stay, with low floor and ceiling effects58.
 Diaphragm function measured by ultrasound (every three days). Although volitional maximal
inspiratory pressure (PImax) is frequently used as a primary outcome measure in IMT studies, and may
improve after IMT14,15,17-19,22 it is neither robust, clinically-relevant nor patient-centred. PImax has
similarities with the manoeuvres used during IMT, therefore improvements can be the result of a

secdrp__001_t.text:
learning
effect of the intervention, rather than a true improvement in diaphragm strength59. Furthermore, only a
weak correlation exists between PImax and non-volitional measures of specific diaphragm strength60. A
diaphragm thickening fraction ((end-inspiratory thickness − end-expiratory thickness)/end-expiratory
thickness)) <29% detects diaphragm dysfunction (sensitivity 85%, specificity 88%)61. It also has the ability
to predict weaning outcome38,61 and avoids the need for costly and technically-challenging invasive
measurements38,61.
I have previously conducted diaphragm ultrasound measurements in mechanically
ventilated patients. Dr Hatem Soliman (consultant intensivist, Harefield Hospital), an expert in diaphragm
ultrasound will provide comprehensive training.
Advice was sought from a health economist who advised cost-effectiveness of the intervention should not be
measured in this study. However, the feasibility of collecting secondary outcomes measures such as the EQ-5D-
3L would help inform cost-effectiveness in the definitive RCT.
Inclusion criteria
As per study 1.
Exclusion criteria
 Undrained pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum
 Pregnancy
 Considered unlikely to survive,

secdrp__001_u.text:
or consultant assessment that the patient is not otherwise appropriate.
Consent procedures
As study 1
Sample size
The primary aim of the feasibility RCT is to estimate rate design parameters for the planning of a large-scale
confirmatory RCT. With a sample size of 50, as recommended for feasibility studies with this aim62 we will be able
to estimate an overall drop-out rate to within a 95% confidence interval of at most +/-14%. This is based on a Wald
confidence interval which is widest around a 50% estimate.
I recruited 5-6 patients a month and performed 1-7 three-hour studies per participant in my previous ICU IMT study
at the Royal Brompton and Harefield hospitals45 (42 beds). The participants’ median duration on the study was 12
days before weaning of mechanical ventilation. The addition of the ICUs at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospitals and
the Lane Fox Unit (68 beds) gives a larger pool of patients to recruit from in this study. With consideration of the
training element of this award and different measurements conducted, I anticipate I can recruit and measure 4-5
participants per month over a 14-month period. My previous work shows I am in a strong position to achieve this.
Data

secdrp__001_v.text:
analysis (Months 33-35)
All feasibility outcomes will be analysed using descriptive statistics (frequency, proportion and 95% CI). Between-
group differences in clinical (secondary) outcomes will not be estimated. Instead, clinical outcomes will be
analysed descriptively (and visually) to inform, in particular, the sample size calculation of a large-scale RCT. The
proportion of missing clinical data as well as potential reasons for absence will be explored to inform a subsequent
large-scale RCT.
Acceptability of delivering D-IMT
Acceptability of delivering treatment is an important component of the development of complex interventions.
Semi-structured interviews, informed by a phenomenological approach will be undertaken with the costed
physiotherapists (n=3-5) in the study to explore their experience of delivering D-IMT. Interviews will be audio-
recorded, transcribed verbatim, anonymised and analysed using Braun and Clarke’s six-step Reflexive Thematic
Analysis phases53.
Equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI)
I am committed to research that actively supports and promotes EDI; every patient eligible to take part in the
proposed research should have the same opportunity to particip

secdrp__001_w.text:
ate. I have collaborated with the research EDI lead
at GSTT in designing this proposal and will meet with them once per year, supplemented with email. The NIHR
INCLUDE project guidelines and the Trust EDI impact assessment tool identified specific areas to improve
participation opportunities, including: enabling surrogate approval for people who lack capacity to consent for
themselves; overcoming language barriers by allocating funding for interpretation services for consent and
research interventions; ensuring patient-facing materials and qualitative data collection tools are accessible by
measuring readability scores, and by PPI group review. Relevant EDI data will be collected for all screened
individuals and recorded on REDCap.
What do you intend to produce from your research?
Outputs for each study will be published open access in peer-reviewed journals to inform both physiotherapy and
multidisciplinary colleagues of the findings. The most appropriate target journals will be discussed after analysis
but due to the novel nature of this work and potential impact on practice, we would aim for journals such as the
American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine or Criti

secdrp__001_x.text:
cal Care. Abstracts will be submitted to the
European Conference on Weaning and Rehabilitation, European Respiratory Society Respiratory Failure and
Mechanical Ventilation Conference and national conferences. Abstracts will ensure the findings are shared widely
and open opportunities for networking, discussion with world leaders, and future collaborations. Data will be
managed within the Open Science Framework to maximise the integrity and value of the work.
How will you inform and engage service users and the wider population?
I will work closely with the Trust PPI lead and the project’s PPI group to facilitate engagement with service users,
services and the wider population. Results will be disseminated through the following:
 Participants and relatives from both studies will be invited to an in-person and video-conference feedback
event to present and discuss findings.
 A lay summary developed with the project’s PPI group will be shared in the ICU waiting areas and the
hospital website.
 A dedicated social media account will be utilised to disseminate results to the research community and the
wider population.
 The Brunel annual research festival, which engages with academic

secdrp__001_y.text:
s and the local community.
 I will engage with communication teams at GSTT, NIHR, charities (such as ICU Steps) and professional
bodies, such as the Association for Chartered Physiotherapists in Respiratory Care (ACPRC) to identify
further appropriate forums for dissemination.
How will your outputs enter our health and social care system or society as a whole?
The outputs of this research will be shared widely and freely with clinicians nationally and internationally as per the
dissemination plan. This includes PPI dissemination, peer-reviewed journals and specialist physiotherapy groups.
As an ACPRC editorial board member and a lecturer on the Advanced Clinical Practice MSc programme at Brunel,
I have further opportunities to widely share the findings and change practice, such as enhancing clinicians’
understanding of weaning and patient experience of IMT in ICU.
What further funding or support will be required if this research is successful?
If the feasibility RCT is successful, this programme of research has a clear path towards the design of a robust,
fully powered definitive RCT comparing a diaphragm focussed, acceptable IMT regime to usual care which will
measure its effects

secdrp__001_z.text:
on clinical and patient related outcomes. This will be enabled by applying for funding through
the postdoctoral NIHR ICA pathway or RfPB stream.
What are the possible barriers for further research, development, adoption, and implementation?
Despite a high proportion of physiotherapists using IMT in critical care, barriers to its use still exist. An international
survey37 and my own national survey (interim analysis) show 27 - 40% of physiotherapists report lack of evidence
or clinical benefit prevents them using IMT in ICU. Evidence produced from this fellowship and the post-doctoral
RCT aims to better inform the literature which, if positive outcomes are found, could improve adoption. Lack of
equipment is commonly cited as a barrier to IMT use in ICU; a robust evidence base could help funding requests
and subsequent IMT adoption.
What do you think the impact of your research will be and for whom?
In the short- and medium-term, study 1 will inform physiotherapists of a diaphragm-focussed and acceptable IMT
load. Longer term, study 2 will inform a definitive trial to test the efficacy of this regime. If this regime is successful
at improving outcomes such as weaning time, patients

secdrp__001_{.text:
would benefit from a reduced duration of mechanical
ventilation, ameliorating the negative effects of prolonged ventilation such as disability after discharge, activity
limitations, impaired quality of life and cognitive impairment. Healthcare services would benefit from reduced ICU
bed days and the associated reduced healthcare cost.
Project Management
I have an established supervisory team that I have worked alongside during my charity fellowship and PCAF,
providing a strong history of effective supervision and project management. My supervisory team has significant
experience in ICU research, mixed methods methodology, physiology, point of care ultrasound and previous PhD
supervision. I will meet with my academic supervisors monthly in addition to individual meetings and email
correspondence. I will meet quarterly with all supervisors together. Steering group meetings will be held 1-2 times
per year with me, principal lay advisor and my supervisory team. As the project lead, I will take responsibility for
day-to-day management of the project including: recruitment and consent; data collection and analysis with
support of a statistician; regular updates with key stakeholders (Res

secdrp__001_|.text:
earch and development department, REC,
NIHR, PPI group). GCP guidance will be adhered to throughout the project. I have experience in financial
management of charity and NIHR grants and will be supported by the GSTT research finance department during
this award.
Ethics
Appropriate ethical approvals will be sought via the Health Research Authority, Research Ethics Committee, and
the host organisation. Both studies will be registered on clinicaltrials.gov. The oesophageal catheter used in study
1 is used in clinical and research settings, is CE and UKCA marked and replaces the participant’s NG tube. IMT
patients14,23,46.
has been shown to be safe in mechanically ventilated Application for ethical approval of study 1 will
be made as soon as the fellowship decision is communicated, with the aim of commencing the study in the first
month of the fellowship.
Success criteria
Development of a diaphragm-focussed, patient-acceptable IMT regime; completion of all study stages within
budget and timeframe; dissemination of the findings as above; completion of intended training programme;
completion and award of a PhD.

Mitigation 1:
 Study 1 app

 A four-month period for ethical approval of study 2; Appropriate areas of the protocol and IRAS form will

 I have an established working relationship with the trust research and development office who are aware

Mitigation 3: Rigorous methodology; Extensive PPI and speech and language therapist input, detailed

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I worked closely with the Trust PPI lead to form a specific PPI group for my research interest and this project. The
PPI group consists of one relative of an ICU survivor, and six ICU survivors, all of whom experienced prolonged
mechanical ventilation and experienced physical rehabilitation in ICU. Five members performed Inspiratory Muscle
Training (IMT) when mechanically ventilated. Three of these members participated in my previous study, and
therefore have specific experience of performing IMT at four different loads in one research occasion. The
complex medical and psychological burden of being critically unwell means this group has been invaluable in
forming this proposal. PPI was fully funded in my PCAF, and I have met with the group five times in preparation of
this application.
Influence and changes to proposal
The group understood the importance of the proposed research and felt they or their next of kin would consent for
the research. They advised to increase the rest period between IMT loads, as they felt participants would
experience fatigue and breathlessnes

secppi__001_b.text:
s after IMT. The rest would enable them to recover fully, and to participate in
the qualitative data collection.
The group gave specific guidance on the scale used to measure breathing difficultly and unpleasantness by
suggesting simple anchor points using a number rating scale to help cognitively impaired or fatigued patients
understanding of the scale.
The group advised that although placement of the oesophageal catheter can be uncomfortable, they realised the
importance of it for the research, and that a single tube used for both feeding and research would be much more
favourable; the protocol was changed accordingly. The group gave detailed contributions to the plain English
summary.
The group advised heavily on the qualitative data collection and offered first-hand accounts of their changing
cognition throughout their ICU stay. Due to this, they suggested 1-3 research occasions per participant in study 1
to improve the richness of the qualitative data and account for variable engagement. The group gave key words
describing their experience of different IMT loads and breathlessness, words which will be used in the qualitative
data collection ‘toolbox’.
Please describe the ways

secppi__001_c.text:
in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Current PPI group members have found the experience rewarding and productive; five out of the seven members
have agreed to continue the role throughout this fellowship. An additional two members will be recruited in the
coming months.
One member (LK), a previous research participant with first-hand experience of different IMT loads when
mechanically ventilated, has agreed to be PPI lead and will attend steering group meetings to ensure a direct link
between the PPI group and the research team. LK and I will and attend a national ICU conference where he will be
given the opportunity to co-present findings.
Training and support
I will work closely with the Trust PPI lead to ensure I continue to follow the UK standards for public involvement in
research. Briefly, the group works well together and all contributions are valued and respected; individual support
and guidance will be provided by the Trust PPI lead; there is allocated funding for their involvement and to attend
PPI training; I will meet separately with the PPI lead prior to m

secppi__001_d.text:
eetings to discuss findings to support him in his role.
Members of the PPI group have been given a role description. The entire group agreed that meetings will take
place using video conferencing to ensure equal access (all members are computer literate but live in different
geographical locations with varied levels of physical function).
Continued involvement in proposed research
The group will meet three times per year, with email updates between meetings. Specific activities will include:
 Discuss and clarify the qualitative questioning to be used in study 1 (funded in PCAF).
 Advise on study 1 data interpretation.
 Review lay summaries for ethics applications.
 Review all patient facing documents.
 Help produce dissemination material and provide guidance on dissemination methods.
Two members of the initial PPI group are unable to continue participation due to family commitments or medical
concerns, which has resulted in a predominantly white group. I recognise the absence of underserved groups and
am actively working with the Trust PPI lead to increase diversity in our aim to recruit more members, in line with
NIHR’s Under-served Communities Programme.

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
This training plan has been developed using the VITAE researcher development framework with my supervisory
team to build on my existing skills. The proposed project has clear potential for benefiting patients, while enabling
broad training and development for my progression as a clinical academic leader. This training plan will equip me
with the skills and knowledge to conduct the proposed research and subsequent post-doctoral research.
Proposed formal study
As a doctoral researcher at Brunel, I will complete the required progression reviews and training. The formal
training element will primarily be provided by the Brunel Doctoral School and cover topics from the four domains of
the researcher development framework. The following courses will further my development:
 Year one: Doctoral research: getting started; embedding EDI into research; academic resilience; critical
thinking and writing.
 Year two: ANOVAs in SPSS; delivering conference presentations; doctoral research: moving forward.
 Year three: Doctoral research: completing successfully; preparing thesis submission.
As an employee of Guys and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust I h
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
ave access to support, events and
masterclasses run by the Imperial and King’s Clinical Academic Training Office. Their annual research symposium
will give me opportunities to present my work and network.
Additional academic training and development to support my development as a researcher and academic
leader
This plan links to specific methodology, statistics, data analysis and interpretation skills required at key milestones
of my project.
Year one (study 1):
 Introduction to qualitative interviews (University of Oxford, 1 day). Practical and theoretical elements of
qualitative research interviews – refreshes and builds on skills learnt on the Brunel qualitative research
methods MSc module.
Qualitative data analysis: Theory and Practice (National Centre for Research Methods, 1 day): To

develop my skills in qualitative data preparation, coding and identifying themes.
 Mixed Methods Design and Analysis (National Centre for Research Methods, 2 days): Design,
sampling, analysis and integration of qualitative and quantitative methods.
 Facilitation: how to get the most out for your PPI activities (UCL, 2 hours). Will improve my skills in
identifying research priorities and desig
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
ning protocols with PPI members.
 Increasing Participation of Ethnic Minorites in Health and Social Care Research (Centre for Ethnic
Health Research / NIHR, eLearning): I recognise the need to increase my awareness of EDI in research;
this training will help me achieve more diverse ethnic representation in my research.
 Inside a Clinical Trials Unit (Queen Mary University London Pragmatic Clinical Trials Unit, 9 months, 4
hours/month) This extensive course provides learning and mentorship in a live setting. I will gain
experience in the design, conduct and governance of pragmatic clinical trials by observation and one-on-
one tutor supervision (12+ hours). This will equip me with the skills to conduct the feasibility RCT in study
2, and further post-doctoral research.
Year 2 (study 1 and 2):
 Mixed methods analysis and integration for health research (Warwick Medical School, 4 days):
Principles of mixed methods analysis: transformation, synthesis, integration, and analysis.
 Supervised diaphragm ultrasound scans (three months, 4 hours/week): Supervised by Dr Soliman
(Consultant Intensivist, Harefield Hospital). As an internationally renowned expert in ICU point of care
ultrasou
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
nd, Dr Soliman will develop my skills in diaphragm ultrasound in preparation for study 2.
 Randomised controlled trials course: A guide to design, conduct, analysis, interpretation and
reporting (NIHR Oxford Biomedical Research Centre, 5 days): This course will provide me with a
thorough grounding in RCT methodology and the skills to conduct a successful RCT.
Year 3 (study 2):
 Writing and publishing qualitative research (UCL, 1 day): This course will support me in describing and
writing up qualitative data.
 Introduction to linear and logistic regression models (University of Bristol, 5 days). I have some
experience in regression analysis; this course will help with more complex data analysis from study 2 and
beyond.
 Leadership development (NIHR eLearning, 3 hours)
Additional training and development to support my personal and professional development as a
practitioner and practice leader
This training will facilitate my development as a clinical academic and leader in critical care:
 Advanced course in acute respiratory failure and mechanical ventilation (European Society of
Intensive Care Medicine, 2 days) Directly linked to my aspiration to be a leader in ventilator weani
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
ng.
Covers advanced assessment and support of ventilated patients.
 Emerging clinical leaders (King’s fund, 6 days). This course will help my understanding and subsequent
development of my leadership style as a clinical academic. These skills will help improve team
collaboration, wellbeing, and importantly, patient care.
I will attend multidisciplinary conferences to explore up-to-date research and network with clinical academic
leaders.
 Intensive Care Society State of the Art Conference (2026): Clinical learning, dissemination of findings
with PPI lead at national conference.
 10th European Conference on Weaning and Rehabilitation (2026): Clinical learning, networking,
dissemination of findings.
 Association of Chartered Physiotherapists in Respiratory Care (2027): Clinical learning, networking.
 European Respiratory Society Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation Conference (2027):
Clinical learning, networking, dissemination of findings.
Details of practice to be undertaken during the fellowship
My professional practice and development plan is aimed at maintaining and developing my skills to become a
physiotherapy leader and expert in rehabilitation, mechanical vent
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
ilation, and weaning in in critical care. I will
complete a four-month secondment (20%) at the Lane Fox Unit, a world leading centre in ventilator weaning and
rehabilitation, where I will advance my clinical practice with multidisciplinary leaders in the field.
To progress my role as a leading clinical academic I will spend three individual weeks (full-time) at Coventry
University Hospital under the supervision of Professor McWilliams to advance my clinical and academic skillset.
I will visit Professor Daniel Langer in Leuven, Belgium for a research and clinical visit. Professor Langer’s lab
conducts research in rehabilitation and inspiratory muscle training in critical care. He is an international expert on
IMT in critical care, with >100 peer reviewed articles.
Throughout the fellowship I will work clinically at the Brompton and Harefield Hospitals to maintain and develop my
specialist skills in critical care physiotherapy. I will continue supporting colleagues with research and service
development to build research capacity within the clinical team.
Primary Doctoral Supervisor
PRIMARY DOCTORAL SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Vicky MacBean
Institution Brunel University London
Position Senio
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
r Lecturer
Description of support provided
Dr MacBean is an academic physiotherapist with an international profile in the field of respiratory muscle
function and physiology and perception of respiratory effort. She worked clinically in the UK and New Zealand,
specialising in paediatric respiratory and critical care physiotherapy, before moving into academia. She
undertook doctoral and postdoctoral research at King’s College London, working alongside world leaders in the
field (Professors Polkey, John Moxham, Anne Greenough, Nicholas Hart, Dr Gerrard Rafferty). She moved to
Brunel University in 2018 as Lecturer in Physiotherapy, becoming Senior Lecturer in 2023.
Dr MacBean has been instrumental in progressing my clinical academic research career since the planning and
award of my charity fellowship in 2020. I have met with her on a regular basis since, depending on the needs of
the project at the time, and she is the primary supervisor for my PCAF.
It is acknowledged that Dr MacBean’s formal doctoral supervisory experience is still growing. She nevertheless
has extensive experience of formal and informal research supervision (MSc, MRes and MD/PhD), both as a
doctoral/postdoctoral f
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
ellow and as a tenured academic. She is currently or has been a member of the supervisory
team for five doctoral researchers at Brunel, and on progression panels for a further two doctoral researchers. She
has longstanding working relationships with Professors Kilbride (since 2019) and Polkey (since 2009), and the
track record of successful joint project completions and publications attests to the quality of the collaboration and
the supervisory team’s support for Dr MacBean as primary supervisor.
Additional Academic Supervisor(s)
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Cherry Kilbride
Institution Brunel University London
Position Professor of Neurorehabilitation and Applied Clinical Research
Description of support provided
Professor Cherry Kilbride is a physiotherapist and Professor of Neurorehabilitation and Applied Clinical Research
at Brunel University London, and the Lead Allied Health Professional for Research at the Royal Free London
NHS Foundation Trust. Professor Kilbride also has a strategic commitment for the development of AHP academic
clinicians at the Trust.
Professor Kilbride joined Brunel in 2007 with twenty years’ experience of being a respected healthcare
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
leader,
manager and clinician. Professor Kilbride is an expert in mixed methods research with over 70 peer reviewed
publications and has a sustained record of attracting and delivering grants to time and target in excess of £1.8m.
Professor Kilbride has seen seven PhD students through to completion, has six ongoing doctoral students
(including NIHR) and supervised over 50 MSc dissertations. Professor Kilbride’s mentorship experience spans
NIHR Advanced, Doctoral, Predoctoral Fellows to Interns. She has also overseen 7 therapists on the NIHR
Associate Principal Investigator role. Professor Kilbride will provide close mentorship and support to Dr MacBean;
they are co-located at Brunel.
I first met Professor Kilbride in the early stages of my charity fellowship, and she has since helped my progression
as a clinical academic; her capacity as a mentor in my PCAF quickly turned to academic supervisor for my DCAF
due to her expertise in mixed methods research. Since taking this role Professor Kilbride, Dr MacBean, Professor
Polkey and myself have met regularly, proving her capacity to supervise me, and for the supervisory team to
collaborate effectively.

ame Professor Michael Polkey

Consultant physician and honorary professor (Imperial College

Description of support provided
Professor Polkey was appointed as a consultant in respiratory medicine at the Royal Brompton Hospital in 2000.
He is now interim director of research at the Royal Brompton and Harefield Hospitals in addition to being chair of
the care group dealing with home non-invasive ventilation and weaning.
Professor Polkey’s expertise aligns well with my DCAF program of work; he is an expert in respiratory physiology,
beginning with his own thesis in 1997. He now heads the respiratory muscle laboratory at the Royal Brompton
Hospital. His clinical practice involves the assessment of patients with advanced COPD for rehabilitation, complex
weaning in critical care, and home ventilation. Professor Polkey has vast research experience with >400
publications. He is or has been associate editor of the European Respiratory Journal, Thorax and Clinical
Science. He has supervised or co-supervised 34 students to PhD (or occasionally MD), of whom five are now
Professors of Medicine.
Professor Polkey’s extensive ex
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
perience of doctoral supervision and internationally leading research places him
in a strong position to support both the programme of research and the doctoral supervision element. Professor
Polkey fully supports Dr MacBean as my primary supervisor and has offered mentorship and support to her as
required. Professor Polkey has been a core member of my supervisory team since 2020, with a proven capacity
to supervise me throughout this time; we have met every 2-4 weeks for supervision and project meetings with my
supervisory team, and he is extremely responsive to emails, normally responding within hours.

University Hospitals Coventry and Warwickshire NHS Trust and

Coventry University
